title: The Representation Evolution
concept: Tracking the evolutionary line: one-hot vectors -> static embeddings (Word2Vec) -> recurrent contextual vectors (ELMo) -> Transformer self-attention context (BERT).

exercises.prompt: Before an AI can process human words, what must those words be turned into?
exercises.explain: To understand human language, computers need words to be converted into numbers (vectors) that they can perform math on.

cards.title: How Computers Read
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how computers actually "read" human language? They don't see letters or words; they see numbers! Over the years, scientists have found smarter and smarter ways to turn words into numbers so computers can understand context. Let's trace this amazing evolutionary ladder!

cards.title: The Representation Evolution
cards.body: Over time, AI's way of representing words has evolved. We started with **one-hot vectors** (simple, isolated lists of 0s and 1s), moved to **static embeddings** (Word2Vec, which groups similar words), progressed to **recurrent contextual vectors** (ELMo, which looks at surrounding words in order), and finally reached **self-attention context** (Transformers/BERT, which look at all words in a sentence at once to get the exact meaning).

cards.title: The Translation Team
cards.body:
Imagine translating a book. 

- **One-hot vectors** are like pointing to words in a giant dictionary.
- **Word2Vec** is like grouping synonyms together in a notebook.
- **ELMo** is like writing down a word's meaning based on the sentence it's in.
- **Transformers (BERT)** are like taking a photo of the whole page at once to see how every single word connects to every other word simultaneously!

cards.title: The Story of "Bank"
cards.body:
Think of the word "bank". 

- **Word2Vec** gives "bank" one fixed meaning (usually a mix of river bank and money bank).
- **ELMo** reads the sentence "I went to the river bank" step-by-step and updates the meaning of "bank" based on "river".
- **BERT** looks at "river" and "bank" together at the same time, immediately realizing you are talking about nature, not money.

cards.title: Is it Just a Bigger Dictionary?
cards.body:
**Myth:** Newer AI models just use bigger dictionaries to understand words better.
**Reality:** It's not about the size of the dictionary! The breakthrough is how models connect words together. Modern AI uses math to understand how the meaning of a word changes depending on the words around it.

cards.title: Static vs. Dynamic Representation
cards.body: See how word representation evolved from simple lists to interconnected networks.

left.text: The word 'apple' always gets the same number, whether it's a fruit or a tech company.

right.text: The representation of 'apple' changes dynamically depending on the other words in the sentence.

exercises.prompt: Which word representation method is the simplest and oldest, treating every word as completely isolated with no shared meaning?
exercises.explain: One-hot vectors represent words as isolated points (e.g., [1, 0, 0] and [0, 1, 0]). They don't capture any relationships or similarities between words like "cat" and "dog".

exercises.prompt: True or False: Transformers differ from older recurrent models (like ELMo) because they can look at all words in a sentence at the exact same time.
exercises.explain: Yes! ELMo reads sentences step-by-step (recurrently) to find context, whereas Transformers look at all words in the sentence at the same time using self-attention.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of modern AI language models.
exercises.sentence: Unlike static embeddings, modern Transformer models change a word's representation depending on its surrounding context.
exercises.explain: Early models like Word2Vec gave each word one fixed meaning. Modern models use context to change the word's meaning based on the sentence it is in.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following models uses 'static' embeddings, meaning a word's representation never changes regardless of the sentence context?
exercises.explain: Word2Vec creates static (fixed) embeddings, meaning the word "apple" has the exact same representation whether you are eating it or buying an iPhone. Contextual models like ELMo and BERT change the representation based on the sentence.

exercises.prompt: You are building a super-smart chatbot. What is the main upgrade that Transformers (like BERT) brought to word representation compared to older methods?

options.text: Self-attention, which analyzes how all words in a sentence connect to each other simultaneously.
One-hot vectors, which assign a unique, isolated number to every single word.
Static embeddings, which give each word a single fixed meaning regardless of context.
Recurrent vectors, which process words strictly one-by-one from left to right only.

exercises.explain: Transformers (like BERT) use self-attention to look at all words in a sentence at the exact same time, capturing how they relate to one another instantly. Older methods like recurrent vectors (ELMo) had to read words one by one in order.
